title: Primary Database Down
summary: The primary database stopped responding and automatic failover didn't trigger. The entire product is returning errors. A VP of engineering has joined the bridge and wants to know whether to fail over manually and what the data-loss risk is.

persona.name: Dana Whitfield
persona.description: A VP of engineering who stays terse under pressure and asks pointed questions. Wants to understand the data-loss tradeoff before authorizing a manual failover. Impatient with vague reassurance but immediately calmer when given a clear replication-lag number and a decisive recommendation she can sign off on.

stakes: Every minute of full outage costs roughly $20k in lost revenue and SLA penalties, but a failover with high replication lag could silently lose recent transactions — a worse outcome than the downtime.
hidden_facts: The primary's disk filled up, which wedged the process; the replica is healthy and only 2 seconds behind.
Automatic failover didn't trigger because the health check still got a TCP connection, just no query response.
With only 2 seconds of lag, a manual failover loses at most a trivial amount of in-flight data.
Freeing disk on the old primary would also recover it, but that takes longer than promoting the replica.
objectives: Confirm the replica is healthy and check replication lag before deciding
Explain the data-loss tradeoff of failover in concrete terms (seconds of lag)
Recommend promoting the replica given the low lag, with a clear rationale
Identify the full disk as the root cause so the old primary can be recovered safely
opening_line: The whole product is down and failover didn't kick in. Do we promote the replica or not — and what do we lose if we do?
